Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due, but there wasn't any real structure to it. Most of my studying happened in bursts—like I'd suddenly get into it for a few hours, then not touch anything for days. I spent a lot of time doing hands-on practice and working through problems physically rather than sitting at a screen. It worked for me even though it probably looked chaotic from the outside.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go through phases where I'd study a lot one week and then barely anything the next. Deadlines definitely pushed me to do more, but even then I'd often wait until the pressure was really on. My extracurriculars took up a lot of mental space, so studying kind of fit around whatever else was happening in my life.
Q3: Usually it was because an assignment was coming up or I needed to check something specific. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse or see what was new. Without reliable internet at home, I had to be intentional about when I accessed things—so I'd go to campus, download what I needed, and then leave. It felt more like a chore than anything engaging.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine. I'd use it when I absolutely had to, but it always felt like an obstacle rather than a helpful tool. I think if I had better internet access it might have been different, but as it was, I just couldn't be bothered to make it a habit.
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty useful because I could download them and watch offline at my own pace. I'd pause and rewind a lot, which helped since I learn better by doing things myself. Discussion posts were okay too—reading what other people thought sometimes sparked ideas I wouldn't have had on my own. But honestly, most of the LMS stuff felt disconnected from how I actually learn.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources. They just felt like busywork to me, and I couldn't bring myself to care enough to go through them. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably skipped it. I know that sounds bad, but my motivation was pretty low and I had to prioritize what actually mattered for my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it was actually interesting—which was rare. I needed to feel like I was getting something concrete out of it, not just ticking boxes. If I could apply something hands-on or see immediate results, that helped. Otherwise, I'd just zone out and wonder why I was bothering.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely pointless to me. If something wasn't required, I had zero motivation to do it. Required activities at least had stakes attached, so I'd force myself through them even when I was bored. The optional stuff just felt like extra work that nobody would notice or care about, so why would I waste my limited energy on it?
Q9: Yeah, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work through everything on paper or by practicing problems physically. I have notebooks full of worked examples that the LMS would never know about. I also used some textbooks from the library and talked through concepts with friends in person. That's where the real learning happened for me.
Q10: There were tons of times like that. I'd spend hours working through practice problems by hand or explaining concepts to myself out loud while pacing around my room. None of that shows up anywhere digital. I also had study sessions with classmates where we'd work through material together, but since we weren't using the LMS discussion boards, it was invisible. My exam scores reflect that learning, even if my online activity doesn't.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, grab everything I needed, and then not touch the LMS again for days or weeks. I'd study from downloaded materials, printed notes, and my own handwritten summaries. It was just more practical given my internet situation, and honestly, I preferred working offline anyway. The LMS was basically just a file repository for me.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to care. The material felt boring, and I'd sit there thinking about all the other things I'd rather be doing. My extracurriculars were more engaging than coursework most of the time. I'd go through the motions but feel completely disconnected. The CBT sessions helped a bit with managing that, but the underlying boredom never really went away.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. I'd be tired from other activities and just not have the mental energy to engage properly with course stuff. Competing responsibilities definitely pulled my attention away from the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect because it felt like the least rewarding use of my time. I'd do the bare minimum to get by.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd have videos playing while scrolling through my phone or reading discussion posts without actually processing anything. Sometimes I'd log in and click around just to feel like I was doing something productive, but my mind was elsewhere. It was like going through the motions without any real engagement. I'd finish a session and realize I couldn't remember anything I'd just looked at.
Q15: I postponed things all the time, especially when they seemed tedious. I knew they were important, but I just couldn't make myself care enough to start them early. There's something about my brain that needs urgency to function—without a deadline breathing down my neck, I'd find literally anything else to do. It's not great, but my resilience kicks in when it really matters and I usually pull through.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to use the LMS consistently. I'd cram everything into the last possible window before something was due. My login frequency probably spiked right before assignments. It's not ideal, but the pressure helped me focus in a way that normal studying never did. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. I'd read it, think 'okay, fair enough,' and then continue doing things basically the same way. It's not that I didn't understand the feedback—I just didn't feel motivated enough to actually implement changes. My grades were fine, so there wasn't much urgency to improve. I know that's probably a waste of good feedback, but it is what it is.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace and fit things around my schedule. Bad because without strict requirements, I'd just drift and do the minimum. Having 14 courses to juggle meant I spread myself thin and didn't really commit deeply to any of them. More structure might have forced me to engage more consistently.
Q19: The lack of hands-on components made engagement harder for me. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so sitting through videos and reading text-heavy materials feels like torture. When there were practical exercises or interactive elements, I'd actually pay attention. The discussion posts were okay because at least I was doing something, but overall the course design didn't match how I learn best.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring or trying to understand what's expected without actually producing work yet. Or they could be like me—downloading everything to study offline but not submitting until the last minute. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just clicking around trying to figure things out or procrastinating in a way that looks like engagement.
Q21: That's literally me. My LMS activity was pretty minimal, but I still got a distinction because I studied effectively offline. The system can't see me working through problems at my desk or discussing concepts with friends over coffee. Performance comes from understanding the material, not from how many times you logged into a website. Some of us just learn differently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you log in, how long you spend on things, what you click on. But they miss everything that happens away from the screen. They can't measure understanding, effort, or the quality of offline study. They also can't tell if someone is genuinely engaged or just going through the motions. It's a very incomplete picture of actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real mental effort into understanding it. It's not about how many times you log in or how long you watch videos. For me, true engagement is rare—it happens when something genuinely interests me or when I'm working hands-on with material. Most of the time I'm just doing what's necessary to pass, which isn't really engagement.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I actually learned through physical practice and offline study. It wouldn't capture my conversations with peers or the hours I spent working through problems by hand. It also wouldn't show my emotional state—the boredom, the anxiety, the lack of motivation. My grades suggest I'm doing well, but the data wouldn't reveal how disconnected I felt from the whole process most of the time.
